results.possibleIdentity: Thomas Burke (often referred to as Tom Burke) was a technical coordinator, multimedia collaborator, and staff member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during its most active period in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He played a vital role in the organization's mission to bridge the gap between the arts and scientific communities. Burke served as the Director of Technical Services for E.A.T. in New York, where he managed the Technical Services Program, a matchmaking service that paired artists with engineers and scientists to realize technologically complex artworks. He was a central figure in the production of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, serving as a project coordinator and technical assistant for the multidisciplinary team. Additionally, he is credited as a technical assistant for Robert Rauschenberg's performance "Open Score" during the seminal "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering" event in 1966. His work in this period was foundational to the development of early electronic and multimedia art.

results.possibleIdentity: Thomas P. Abeles (often referred to as Tom Abeles) is a scientist, futurist, and educator who has been active in the intersection of art, science, and technology since the mid-1960s. He gained early recognition in 1966 for co-authoring papers in 'Nature' on neutron activation analysis as applied to archaeology and the study of ancient potsherds, demonstrating a career-long interest in using scientific techniques to analyze cultural and artistic objects. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he was involved in environmental and energy technology, including work on methane digesters and solar film festivals. His association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is likely as a technical collaborator or member, consistent with E.A.T.'s mission to pair engineers and scientists with artists. In later years, Abeles became a prominent figure in 'serious games' and educational technology, serving as the long-time editor of the journal 'On the Horizon' and contributing to projects with ETC Press (associated with Carnegie Mellon University) that explore 'Art and Technology' in virtual theater and games. He has also written on the aesthetics and ontological problems of computer art.

Julian T. Abeles (died 1973) was a prominent American music attorney who was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. While not a primary artist, he was a central figure in the arts industry, specifically representing major music publishers and organizations such as the Music Publishers' Protective Association (MPPA). He was often cited in trade publications like 'Billboard' and 'Variety' for his role in high-profile copyright litigation and efforts to regulate the music industry. He was once referred to as the 'Czar of Jazz' by a bandleaders' organization for his influence in the music business.
